policy_statement: Evaluate a program for reducing GHG emissions for new development that require General Plan amendments.
mechanism_description: The policy evaluates a program to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from new development tied to General Plan amendments, supporting an emissions-reduction pathway.
primary_causal_pathway: Policy evaluation + program design → strengthened/implemented development GHG requirements → ↓ energy/fuel use and/or ↓ embodied carbon → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city assesses and refines a program applied to new development (especially projects requiring General Plan amendments), potentially altering planning approvals, building performance requirements, mitigation measures, and compliance pathways.  
2) Mediating physical/process mechanism: Improved program design and set leads to lower operational energy demand (efficiency, electrification, better envelopes), lower-carbon energy supply (renewables procurement), and/or lower embodied emissions (materials choices), thereby reducing combustion and upstream emissions.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced CO₂e emissions attributable to new development over time (avoided lock-in of high-emitting built form).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is enabling and tightening development-related measures that reduce greenhouse gas emissions from new buildings/infrastructure.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance action (evaluation) is in service of implementing or strengthening measures that reduce emissions from new development; the main outcome is ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “evaluation” is an administrative step, its purpose is explicitly GHG reduction; it is not primarily about coping with hazards (adaptation), using fewer non-energy resources as the main endpoint (resource efficiency), or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the evaluated program were purely procedural with no implementation or focused mainly on climate-risk resilience for new development, adaptation could dominate; however, the stated intent is emissions reduction, so mitigation remains primary.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that evaluates/updates a development GHG-reduction program as part of an emissions-reduction pathway has the same dominant intended effect—driving down development-related CO₂e—so mitigation is the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the downstream measures prioritize operational efficiency, electrification, renewable procurement, embodied carbon, or offsets must be determined from policy-specific evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Evaluation-only language can weaken causal certainty; classification assumes the evaluation is used to implement or strengthen development emissions requirements rather than being a non-binding study. If the program centers on adaptation/resilience standards for development, recategorization may be warranted.
key_indicators: "Evaluate a program"; "reducing GHG emissions"; "new development"; "require General Plan amendments"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could become a regulatory-mandate or performance-standard if the evaluation results in adopted requirements for new development, but the stated action itself is only evaluation/design (no explicit enforceable standard yet).
